pattern_recognition: Two Pointers Technique; this problem is a classic example of using two pointers to find a pair that sums to a target in a sorted array. The sorted nature of the array signals that we can efficiently narrow down our search space.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every possible pair of numbers to see if they sum to the target.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through each element and for each element, iterate through the remaining elements to find a pair that sums to the target.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize the sorted property of the array by employing two pointers, one at the start and one at the end, to find the target sum more efficiently.
approaches.algorithm: Initialize two pointers, one at the beginning and one at the end of the array. If the sum of the values at these pointers is equal to the target, return their indices. If the sum is less than the target, move the left pointer to the right; if greater, move the right pointer to the left.

dry_run: Consider the input array [2, 7, 11, 15] and target 9. Start with two pointers: left at index 1 (value 2) and right at index 4 (value 15). The sum is 17, which is greater than 9, so move the right pointer left to index 3 (value 11). The sum is now 13, still greater than 9, so move the right pointer left again to index 2 (value 7). Now the sum is 9, which matches the target. Return indices 1 and 2.
edge_cases: An empty input array should return an indication of no solution. A single element array should also return no solution since a pair cannot be formed. Duplicates in the array should be handled correctly, ensuring the same element is not counted twice unless allowed. Large input sizes should be tested for performance, and potential overflow should be considered when summing large integers.
common_pitfalls: 1. Forgetting to return 1-indexed results instead of 0-indexed. 2. Not handling cases where no valid pair exists, leading to incorrect outputs. 3. Mismanaging pointer movements, which could lead to infinite loops or missed pairs.
